questions.prompt: Who owns each side of the customer-onboarding split?

choices.label: Tech owns everything technical; senior only handles customer comms.
choices.rationale: Conflates 'technical' with 'committal.' Subscription and product-mix choices are committal even though they don't involve menu clicks.

choices.label: Senior owns the wizards; tech owns the policy.
choices.rationale: Inverted. The wizards are runbook work (tech); policy at the org level is above the helpdesk floor.

choices.label: Huntress support owns onboarding; the MSP doesn't drive it.
choices.rationale: Onboarding is the MSP's work. Huntress support fields product questions, not delivery decisions.

questions.explanation: Senior owns decisions that commit the customer (subscription, product mix, deployment strategy, permanent org deletion). Tech work is procedural; senior work is committal. The central frame of the lesson.

questions.prompt: "If the runbook doesn't cover it, I should improvise." Why is that wrong?
choices: A runbook gap is a senior question, not a tech improvisation.

choices.label: Because Huntress support has to approve every off-runbook action.
choices.rationale: Huntress support isn't the body that approves delivery deviations.

choices.label: Because off-runbook actions void the customer's contract.
choices.rationale: Overclaim. The issue is operational drift, not contract voiding.

choices.label: Improvising is fine as long as you document it after.
choices.rationale: Documenting after doesn't reframe an unauthorised commitment. The decision goes to the senior first.

questions.explanation: Improvising creates undocumented customer-specific deviations the next tech won't know about. Improvising creates undocumented customer-specific deviations that future techs and seniors don't know about. The decision belongs above.

questions.prompt: Which of these onboarding questions arrives in tech-shape but carries a decision the senior owns?

choices.label: "Which menu do I click to add a notification address?"
choices.rationale: Pure portal action, covered by the runbook. Tech work.

choices: "Should we deploy to the workstations as well as the servers?"

choices.label: "Where do I find the agent installer for Windows?"
choices.rationale: Documentation lookup, not a decision. Tech work.

choices.label: "Which RMM job runs the EDR push?"
choices.rationale: Runbook lookup, not a customer commitment. Tech work.

questions.explanation: Deployment-strategy questions arrive in tech-shape but commit the customer to scope and billing. Bump before clicking.

questions.prompt: Mid-onboarding, the customer's IT manager mentions: "Oh, and we have about a dozen Linux servers we'd like covered too. Can you grab those while you're at it?" The runbook only mentions the workstation OU. What do you do?

choices.label: Add the Linux servers to the rollout, Linux is supported by the agent.
choices.rationale: Scope change on a verbal request. Billing implications (per-endpoint pricing) plus delivery-process implications (undocumented additions confuse later support).

choices: It's a good addition but it's a deployment-strategy question.

choices.label: Add the Linux servers and flag it in the ticket so the senior catches it on review.
choices.rationale: Action before decision. Even if caught on review, the customer now expects coverage and the conversation gets harder. Bump first, click second.

questions.explanation: Bump to a senior, confirm scope, add to the next rollout window. Route the decision to the layer that commits to it. You're not refusing; you're bumping.

questions.prompt: The senior replies: "Linux is in scope per their contract, fine to deploy, but check the OU before pushing, not all twelve are production." What do you do?

choices.label: Push to all twelve as the runbook directs.
choices.rationale: Ignores the qualifier in the senior's response, same family of mistake as acting on Evidence instead of Recommendation.

choices: Confirm with the IT manager which servers are production.

choices.label: Push to all twelve and mark four for 'review later' in your notes.
choices.rationale: 'Review later' is a quiet way to say 'I committed to coverage I wasn't authorised to commit to.' Non-production servers wait for the senior's decision.

questions.explanation: Push to those, document the decision and the excluded set. You did what the senior asked, documented the exclusion, and didn't expand beyond authorised scope.

questions.prompt: The lesson uses the word "bump" to describe an escalation. What does it mean in practice?
choices: A one-line message to the senior.

choices.label: A formal ticket transfer that pauses all work for 24 hours.
choices.rationale: Overstates the cost. A bump is meant to keep the work moving.

choices.label: A Huntress support ticket asking the vendor to confirm the action.
choices.rationale: Vendor support isn't the right body for delivery decisions; the MSP senior is.

choices.label: A note in the customer's record to discuss at the next quarterly review.
choices.rationale: Too slow, onboarding decisions need answers in hours, not quarters.

questions.explanation: ("customer wants X, sounds like a product-mix question, can you confirm?"); Most respond inside the hour and the work moves on. Not an apology, not a stop-everything escalation. A clarifying ping that keeps the work moving.

questions.prompt: "The senior will be annoyed if I bump every product-mix question." What's the right framing?
choices: Annoyance from a quick "should I do X?" is fleeting;

choices.label: Bumping is always welcome; seniors love being asked everything.
choices.rationale: Overclaim. Seniors do get annoyed by trivial questions a runbook would answer.

choices.label: Reduce bumping by guessing at the answer if you're 80% sure.
choices.rationale: Exactly the failure mode the lesson warns against.

choices.label: Save all product-mix questions for the weekly check-in.
choices.rationale: Some are time-sensitive (customer is waiting on a call). If reversibility or understanding is 'no,' bump now.

questions.explanation: Annoyance from a contract problem caused by an unauthorised commitment is permanent. The cost of a quick clarifying question is small and fleeting. The cost of a customer commitment you weren't authorised to make is durable.

questions.prompt: The two-question test (reversible? understand why?) helps you decide which onboarding questions to bump now versus capture for the check-in. How does it apply?
choices: If either answer is "no," bump now.

choices.label: If both answers are "yes," bump now to confirm. Otherwise proceed.
choices.rationale: Inverted. The test catches the irreversible or poorly-understood cases.

choices.label: The two-question test doesn't apply to onboarding.
choices.rationale: It does, exactly as the filter for 'bump now vs. capture for check-in.'

choices.label: Run the test only after you've already touched the wizard.
choices.rationale: Backwards, the test runs before the action, not after.

questions.explanation: Otherwise the question can sit on the running list for the next check-in. The test catches the irreversible or poorly-understood cases. Reversible-and-understood can wait for the check-in.

questions.prompt: A senior says "you bumped that question to me, but you could have just done it." What's the right way to respond?
choices: Note the feedback, ask which class of action they'd like the next bump for.

choices.label: Apologise and commit to never bumping that class of question again.
choices.rationale: Over-correction. Bumping was the right reflex for the ceiling category; learn the boundary, don't abandon the rule.

choices.label: Stop bumping anything ambiguous from now on; the senior just told you to.
choices.rationale: Strong over-correction. Onboarding still has irreversible and committal categories that always bump.

choices.label: Push back and explain why bumping was correct.
choices.rationale: Defensive. The conversation is more useful if you treat the senior's feedback as a chance to refine the boundary together.

questions.explanation: Adjust the runbook if there's a category that's been under-defined. Treat the senior's feedback as a chance to refine the boundary, not to recalibrate silently or to capitulate entirely.

questions.prompt: Permanently deleting an offboarded organisation from the Huntress portal is a tech action once the offboarding sequence completes.
questions.explanation: Permanent deletion is irreversible and senior-owned. Most MSPs leave the org archived for record-retention reasons regardless of when the offboarding sequence completes.
